description: Finance & accounting grounding for the accounting/finance lens — the valuation, risk, and bookkeeping principles. Educational reference, not financial advice.

entries.title: Time value of money
entries.summary: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow because it can earn return. Present value PV = FV/(1+r)^n discounts future cash flows to today; future value compounds them forward. This single idea underlies NPV, bond pricing, loan amortization, and retirement planning — the foundation of all valuation.

entries.title: NPV & IRR
entries.summary: Net Present Value sums a project's discounted cash flows minus its cost; NPV>0 means it creates value at the chosen discount rate. The Internal Rate of Return is the discount rate that makes NPV zero — the project's implied yield. NPV is the theoretically correct decision rule; IRR is intuitive but can mislead with non-conventional cash flows or mutually exclusive projects.

entries.title: Double-entry bookkeeping
entries.summary: Every transaction posts equal debits and credits so the accounting equation Assets = Liabilities + Equity always balances. Debits raise assets/expenses; credits raise liabilities/equity/revenue. This self-checking structure (the trial balance must net to zero) is the 500-year-old backbone that makes financial statements auditable.

entries.title: The three financial statements
entries.summary: The income statement shows profit over a period (revenue − expenses); the balance sheet is a snapshot of what's owned vs owed (assets = liabilities + equity); the cash-flow statement reconciles accrual profit to actual cash across operating/investing/financing. They interlock — net income flows to retained earnings, and cash-flow ties the two — so all three must be read together.

entries.title: Accrual vs cash accounting
entries.summary: Accrual accounting records revenue when earned and expenses when incurred (the matching principle), regardless of cash timing; cash accounting records only when money moves. Accrual gives a truer picture of performance (and is required for most firms) but means profit ≠ cash — a profitable company can still run out of cash, which is why the cash-flow statement exists.

entries.title: Risk, return & diversification
entries.summary: Higher expected return demands higher risk. Diversification reduces portfolio risk because imperfectly correlated assets' fluctuations partly cancel — the only 'free lunch.' Total risk splits into systematic (market-wide, undiversifiable) and idiosyncratic (asset-specific, diversifiable); only systematic risk is rewarded, the core insight behind modern portfolio theory.

entries.title: CAPM & cost of capital
entries.summary: The Capital Asset Pricing Model prices systematic risk: expected return = risk-free rate + β·(market premium), where β measures sensitivity to market moves. It gives the cost of equity, which combines with the after-tax cost of debt into the WACC — the discount rate used in NPV. Garbage discount rate, garbage valuation.

entries.title: Compound interest & the rule of 72
entries.summary: Compounding earns return on prior return, making growth exponential: more frequent compounding and longer horizons dominate outcomes. The rule of 72 estimates doubling time as 72/r% (8% ≈ 9 years). The same exponential math is why high-interest debt spirals and why early retirement saving outperforms larger late contributions.

entries.title: Liquidity vs solvency
entries.summary: Liquidity is the ability to meet short-term obligations (current ratio, quick ratio); solvency is the ability to meet long-term obligations (debt-to-equity, interest coverage). A firm can be solvent but illiquid (assets exist but aren't cash now) — the classic cause of otherwise-healthy businesses failing in a crunch.

entries.title: Depreciation & amortization
entries.summary: Capital assets are expensed over their useful life rather than all at once — depreciation for tangible assets, amortization for intangibles — to match cost against the revenue they help generate. Methods (straight-line vs accelerated) shift the timing of expense and taxable income; it's a non-cash expense, which is why it's added back in cash-flow analysis.
